title: Quantum memory and scrambling from the perspective of a classical neural network
authors: Dimitrios Maroulakos, Andrzej Wal, Marcin Kowalik, Czesław Jasiukiewicz, Rohit Kumar Shukla, Sunil K. Mishra, Levan Chotorlishvili
abstract: Entropic uncertainty relations are universal quantifiers of fundamental uncertainties of quantum measurements and are widely discussed in the quantum metrology literature. Quantum memory is a phenomenon related to the specific type of quantum correlations that allows for reducing fundamental uncertainties of quantum measurements. In the present work, the modified concept of quantum memory for time-dependent problems is proposed. We compare the time-dependent formulation of quantum memory with the out-of-time-ordered correlator (OTOC). Quantum memory is a rigorous mathematical concept that requires demanding calculations. Thus, until now, quantum memory has been discussed mainly for simple model systems and stationary problems. In the present work, we demonstrate that quantum memory can also be studied for realistic and physically relevant systems, e.g., the atomic helical spin chain, as well as the emergence and propagation of quantum correlations in time. We found that quantum memory manifests faster oscillations in time than OTOC and does not equilibrate. Furthermore, an artificial neural network is trained and asked to predict results for OTOC and quantum memory. These results show that quantum memory is more sensitive than OTOC in terms of broken inversion symmetry and the nonreciprocal effect.
summary: This paper explores how quantum information scrambles and how quantum memory evolves in a chiral spin chain. By comparing the Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlator (OTOC) with a modified quantum memory formalism, the authors show that quantum memory is a more sensitive probe of broken inversion symmetry. They also demonstrate that a classical artificial neural network can be trained to predict these complex quantum dynamics, although the prediction error increases as the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction becomes stronger.
main_problem: The study investigates the time-dependent dynamics of quantum memory and information scrambling (OTOC) in chiral spin chains, specifically looking at how broken inversion symmetry affects these processes.
main_result: Quantum memory is found to be more sensitive to broken inversion symmetry and the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction than OTOC, exhibiting faster oscillations and a lack of equilibration. Additionally, a classical neural network can predict these dynamics, though its accuracy decreases with stronger DMI.
method: The authors use exact diagonalization and analytical derivations for single and two-excitation sectors, combined with a classical deep learning artificial neural network (ANN) trained via backpropagation to predict time-series results.
model_or_system: A 1D chiral spin-1/2 chain (modeled after multiferroic materials or atoms on Ir(001) surfaces) featuring nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic (J1) and next-nearest-neighbor antiferromagnetic (J2) interactions, along with a Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction term.
key_observables: Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlator (OTOC), Quantum Memory (via Entropic Uncertainty Relations), von Neumann entropy, and multipartite entanglement (CKW monogamy inequality).
important_parameters: Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction strength (D), exchange constants (J1, J2), and the number of excitations.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to specific excitation sectors (single and two-excitation) and periodic boundary conditions; the neural network is a classical architecture, not a quantum one.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup of an atomic chain; Figure 2 and 3 illustrate the neural network architecture; Figure 4 plots the time-dependent reduction in measurement uncertainty; Figure 5 shows OTOC dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of time-dependent quantum memory, defines the chiral spin chain Hamiltonian, presents the analytical and numerical methods for calculating OTOC and entropy, describes the ANN training protocol, and concludes with a comparison of the sensitivity of different observables and the predictive performance of the network.
why_interesting: This paper is relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems because it explores how broken symmetries and chiral interactions influence the propagation of information and the stability of quantum correlations, providing a bridge between classical machine learning and quantum information diagnostics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The proposed model mimics the experimental setup of the work [17]. The atomic chain with a length of up to 30 nm, formed on the Ir(001) surface [18]. The spins are coupled by the nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic and the next- nearest-neighbor antiferromagnetic interactions. Competing exchange interaction terms lead to spin frustration. The Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction between nearest spins stabilizes the chiral spin order. When studying the effect of quantum memory, we perform two measurements of the qubit A and measure the Z and X components of the spin. When studying OTOC, we consider two local perturbations ˆW = ˆσz A = ˆσz 1 and ˆV = ˆσz B = ˆσz L/2, where L is the...
Figure 2: The pictorial plot of the artificial neurons which are used to predict the time-dependence of OTOC and quantum memory.

Figure 3: Schematic of an ANN used to predict OTOC C(t) and quantum memory, for given values of exchange constants J1, J2, and DM constant D. The ANN consists of two hidden dense layers L0 and L1 with 64 and 32 neurons, respectively. The output layer contains 25 neurons.

Figure 4: Plot for the left hand side (amber) and the right hand side (blue), as functions of time for the inequality S+(X|B)+S+(Z|B) ≥log2(1/c)+S+(A|B), (8), where c = 1/2 is a measure of complementarity and S+(X|B), S+(Z|B) are conditional quantum entropies of the states (6), (7), |ψ1,2⟩≡|0⟩A , |1⟩A and |ϕ1,2⟩= 1 √

Figure 5: Plot for C+(t), in (12) and the first excited state n1 = 1, where we take |φni⟩≡ni. The spin chain has length, L = 10 and perform summation over all states n2, n3, n4. For the coefficient An3n4 n1n2, use expressions in (46)-(49), taking into account (4).For phase we use (50). The time unit is set by |J1|/ℏ.

Figure 6: OTOC as a function of time for two excitation case. The number of the spins L = 10 (6a) and L = 20 (6b). Figure 6c and Figure 6d are the same but plotted for a shorter time interval. The system parameters are J1 = −1, J2 = 1, D = 0.5. The time unit is set by |J1|/ℏ.

Figure 7: OTOC as a function of time for two excitation case and nearest neighbor coupling. The number of the spins L = 10 (7a) and L = 20 (7b). Figures 7c, 7d are the same but plotted for a shorter time interval. The system parameters are J1 = −1, J2 = 0, D = 0.5. The time unit is set by |J1|/ℏ.

Figure 8: The exact (ground truth) and the predicted via neural network values of OTOC as a function of time. For particular values of parameters specified in the captions of corresponding figures, respectively. As we see, the maximal values of the exact and predicted OTOCs are synchronized in time and show good qualitative agreement for all sets of parameters.

Figure 9: The time-dependent quantum memory for anticlockwise (LHS) propagating spin excitations. The values of the parameters: J1 = −0.90, J2 = 0.90, D = 0.50. We see growth in time mismatch between exact and predicted by neural network peaks of the quantum memory. The result for clockwise (RHS) propagating spin excitations manifests the same trend (not shown).

Figure 10: The time-dependent quantum memory for anticlockwise (LHS) propagating spin excitations. The values of the parameters: J1 = −0.90, J2 = 0.90, D = 0.0. When DM interaction is zero D = 0, the mismatch between the exact and predicted via the neural network peaks and phases of the quantum memory signal are smaller than in the nonzero DM case. The result for clockwise (RHS) propagating spin excitations manifests the same trend (not shown).
